1.synthesis: God commands Moses to turn back toward the sea — an apparently irrational military position — between Migdol and the sea, before Baal-zephon (vv.1–2). God explains: Pharaoh will think Israel is 'wandering in the land' and shut in by the wilderness; God will harden his heart to pursue, 'and I will get glory over Pharaoh and all his host, and the Egyptians shall know that I am the Lord' (v.4). Pharaoh's heart turns; he takes 600 chosen chariots and pursues Israel. The Israelites see the approaching army and cry out in terror; they turn on Moses: 'Is it because there are no graves in Egypt that you have taken us away to die in the wilderness?' (v.11). Moses responds: 'Fear not, stand firm, and see the salvation of the Lord, which he will work for you today. For the Egyptians whom you see today, you shall never see again. The Lord will fight for you, and you have only to be silent' (vv.13–14).
1.historical_context: Matthew Henry on Israel's predicament: 'On the one hand was Pi-hahiroth, a range of craggy rocks impassable; on the other hand were Migdol and Baalzephon... before them was the sea; behind them were the Egyptians — so that there was no way open for them but upwards, and thence their deliverance came.' The specific naming of Baal-zephon ('lord of the north') is a detail that suggests this was a recognized shrine and landmark on Egypt's northeast frontier; God positions Israel directly in front of a pagan deity to demonstrate sovereignty over the very site. Pharaoh's calculation that Israel was 'entangled in the wilderness' (v.3) was the natural military assessment of the situation — and God designed the position to produce exactly that assessment. The mixed response of the Israelites (v.10: some cried to the Lord in prayer; others cried against Moses in murmuring) mirrors the recurring pattern of the wilderness generation that Paul warns against in 1 Corinthians 10:1–12.
1.literary_structure: The section establishes the impossible situation before its resolution. God's statement of purpose (vv.3–4) frames the entire crisis as intentional positioning for a climactic display of divine glory: the hardening of Pharaoh's heart to pursue is itself the instrument by which God's name is proclaimed. Moses's speech (vv.13–14) is the theological masterpiece of the section — possibly the finest statement of faith under pressure in all of Exodus: 'stand firm,' 'see the salvation,' 'the Egyptians you see today you will never see again,' 'the Lord will fight for you.' The imperative 'be silent' is not passivity but trust — the recognition that there is nothing for the creature to do when the Creator is fighting.
1.theological_application: Matthew Henry on Moses's speech: 'We are not to reckon those straits which God brings his people into as tokens of his displeasure against them; rather, God sometimes raises difficulties in the way of salvation that he may have the glory of subduing them.' Gill on 'the Lord will fight for you': 'The battle is God's, not man's; the people are to be spectators, not warriors — the salvation wrought here is wholly divine.' John Wesley: 'We may be in the way of our duty, following God and hastening towards heaven, and yet may be in great straits — troubled on every side.' The pattern of the Red Sea — impossible situation, prayer and complaint mixed, divine command to move forward, total victory — is the pattern of all redemptive crisis: the worse the strait, the more glorious the deliverance, and the more unmistakable that its author is God alone.
1.christology: 'The Lord will fight for you, and you have only to be silent' (v.14): the salvation of the Red Sea is entirely God's work, performed while Israel stands still. Isaiah applies this typologically to the new Exodus: 'In returning and rest you shall be saved; in quietness and trust shall be your strength' (Isaiah 30:15). The salvation wrought at the cross is similarly the act of the divine Warrior while his people stand helpless — 'while we were still sinners, Christ died for us' (Romans 5:8). Israel's cry that they would have been better off in Egyptian slavery (v.12) is the exact temptation of the unregenerate heart to return to bondage rather than face the crisis of the liberation journey — answered by the promise that the enemy they see today they will never see again.

15.synthesis: 'The Lord said to Moses, "Why do you cry to me? Tell the people of Israel to go forward. Lift up your staff, and stretch out your hand over the sea and divide it, that the people of Israel may go through the sea on dry ground"' (vv.15–16). The angel of God moves from the front to the rear of the camp; the pillar of cloud moves to stand between Israel and Egypt — darkness on one side, light on the other (vv.19–20). Moses stretches his hand; all night the Lord drives the sea back with a strong east wind; the water divides, and Israel walks through on dry ground 'with the waters being a wall to them on their right hand and on their left' (v.22). The Egyptians pursue; in the morning watch the Lord in the pillar of fire and cloud looks down and throws the Egyptian forces into panic — chariot wheels clog, officers recognize: 'Let us flee from before Israel, for the Lord fights for them against Egypt!' (v.25).
15.historical_context: Matthew Henry on the dividing of the sea: 'It was a type of baptism (1 Corinthians 10:1–2). Israel's passage through it was typical of the conversion of souls (Isaiah 11:15), and the Egyptians' perdition in it was typical of the final ruin of all impenitent sinners (Revelation 20:14).' Keil and Delitzsch on the mechanics: 'When Moses stretched out his hand... Jehovah made the water go by a strong east wind the whole night, and made the sea into dry ground, and the water split itself — and the Israelites went in the midst of the sea, in the dry, and the water was a wall on the right and on the left.' The east wind is the natural instrument; the miraculous element is the precision, timing, and scale. The pillar's repositioning (vv.19–20) to stand between the camps — giving light to Israel and darkness to Egypt — creates a physical enactment of the spiritual division between the redeemed and the lost. The Egyptians' chariot wheels clogging in the muddy bed (v.25) shows the turning of the tide.
15.literary_structure: The crossing narrative is the central event of the entire Exodus cycle, and its literary construction reflects its weight. Two parallel motions proceed simultaneously all night: Israel advances through the dry sea-bed (eastward), while the pillar repositions to block Egyptian pursuit (westward-facing). When morning comes, the two motions converge: Israel has crossed, and Egypt advances into the trap the sea-bed has become. The Egyptian officers' self-recognition ('the Lord fights for them against Egypt') is the most dramatic conversion of perspective in the narrative — the very enemies admit what Moses said in verse 14. The whole-night timing (east wind all night, watchmen's early morning observation) gives the event an almost liturgical structure: Israel spends the night in the passage of faith, and the morning brings salvation.
15.theological_application: Matthew Henry on Moses's command to move: 'We must not think that praying and doing are inconsistent; when we have prayed for deliverance, we must bestir ourselves and co-operate with providence; Moses was to stretch out his rod, and Israel was to march.' Gill on the water-wall: 'the waters were as a wall to them on their right hand and on their left — a most marvellous thing, which none but the God of nature could effect, and which he did by a miracle beyond any natural means.' Calvin on the Egyptian officers' confession: 'even their enemies are compelled to confess that God is fighting for Israel — the hardest hearts are broken when God's power is exercised in its fullness.' The clogged chariot wheels are a fitting emblem of the way God turns the oppressor's most powerful instrument into the instrument of his own defeat.
15.christology: Paul's typological reading is unambiguous: 'all were baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea' (1 Corinthians 10:2). The Red Sea crossing is the Old Testament's baptism — the transition from the old life (Egypt) to the new (wilderness journey toward the promised land), achieved by passing through the waters that simultaneously liberate the redeemed and destroy their pursuers. Romans 6:4 states the antitype: 'We were buried therefore with him by baptism into death, in order that, just as Christ was raised from the dead by the glory of the Father, we too might walk in newness of life.' The east wind that parts the waters and the word of God that commands them to part are the breath and voice of the Spirit, who overshadows the waters of baptism.

26.synthesis: At God's command Moses stretches his hand over the sea; at morning the waters return. 'The Egyptians fled into it, and the Lord threw the Egyptians into the midst of the sea. The waters returned and covered the chariots and the horsemen; of all the host of Pharaoh that had followed them into the sea, not one of them remained' (vv.27–28). Israel walked through on dry ground; the waters were a wall on both sides (v.29). 'Thus the Lord saved Israel that day from the hand of the Egyptians, and Israel saw the Egyptians dead on the seashore. Israel saw the great power that the Lord used against the Egyptians, so the people feared the Lord and believed in the Lord and in his servant Moses' (vv.30–31).
26.historical_context: Keil and Delitzsch on the mechanics of the return: 'when the east wind which divided the sea ceased to blow, the sea began to flow together... and thus Jehovah shook the Egyptians in the midst of the sea, so that Pharaoh's chariots and horsemen, to the very last man, were buried in the waves.' Josephus reports that the army of Pharaoh consisted of fifty thousand horse and two hundred thousand foot (Adam Clarke), of whom not one remained. Charles Ellicott raises the question of whether Pharaoh himself drowned, noting that Menephthah — the most likely candidate — reigned at least eight years after the Exodus, suggesting the king may not have entered the sea personally. The Egyptians 'fled into' the returning waters (v.27): their confused flight when the wheels clogged drove them not out of the sea-bed but deeper into it, straight into the returning flood. The dead on the seashore (v.30) provided the material for Israel's spoil.
26.literary_structure: The destruction of Egypt is narrated in a single verse (v.28: 'not one of them remained'), the supreme understatement given the size of the force. The literary focus then shifts immediately to Israel: what Israel saw (the Egyptians dead, v.30), what they experienced ('the Lord saved Israel,' v.30), and what they felt and believed (v.31: fear of the Lord and faith in the Lord and Moses). The salvation is complete not when Israel crosses but when Israel sees the enemy destroyed — only then is the pursuit definitively over. The twin response of verse 31 — 'feared the Lord' and 'believed in the Lord and in his servant Moses' — is the narrative's climax: the crossing produces faith. This faith-response connects directly to the Song of Moses that follows (ch.15).
26.theological_application: Matthew Henry: 'Israel saw the Egyptians dead on the seashore — a most marvellous sight, and what fully convinced them that their enemies were utterly cut off, and they should never be troubled with them more.' Gill on 'not one of them remained': 'the destruction was total and irrecoverable; there was no remnant left to carry news of the catastrophe back to Egypt or to pursue Israel further.' Adam Clarke: 'Had the Israelites been intent on plunder or on ambition, how easily might both have been gratified — they might have returned and subjugated the whole land; but they were led by God, and went where he directed.' Matthew Henry on the fear and faith of verse 31: 'The Red Sea was an effectual cure of the Israelites' idolatry in Egypt; after that we never find them hankering after the gods of Egypt... note, remarkable appearances of God's power and justice ought to increase our reverence and our faith.'
26.christology: 'The Lord saved Israel that day from the hand of the Egyptians' (v.30): the word 'saved' (yasha') is the root of both Joshua and Jesus — the Savior's name embedded in the Exodus act. The Egyptians seen dead on the seashore are the type of all powers that pursue the people of God and are finally and permanently overcome: 'For he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet. The last enemy to be destroyed is death' (1 Corinthians 15:25–26). The faith that Israel exercises upon seeing Egypt destroyed (v.31) — 'they believed in the Lord and in his servant Moses' — is the faith that the resurrection produces in the New Testament: the death and destruction of the enemy (sin, death, the devil) is the ground on which resurrection-faith rests. Matthew Henry's observation stands: those who see the enemy destroyed will never hanker after his kingdom again.
